nativeRange.habitat: Clear, shallow waters of outer reef areas, lagoons, back-reef zones, and reef-tops; typically encountered at 3–15 m though the species' overall depth range extends from the surface to about 60 m

diet.notes: Generally best fed a varied carnivore diet of meaty marine foods such as krill, raw shrimp, squid, clam, mussel, and chopped marine fish. Tends to take prepared foods readily once settled and has been known to feed twice daily. Feeder fish are best avoided as a primary food due to nutritional inadequacy and disease risk.

goodWith: large predatory fish of comparable size

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 3348x2511. Vivid scarlet in-situ individual, tack-sharp, whole fish in frame with the diagnostic white/black-edged V (flag) caudal marking clearly visible.
Hectonichus; 1641x1235. Bright red lateral view, sharp and well-lit, whole fish shown with white-edged caudal V-marking; different individual for variety.
NOAA Photo Library; 2726x2412. Sharp near-frontal angle of a reddish-brown individual that clearly displays the twin white V-bands on the tail; adds angle variety.

summary: The Red Flag Grouper (Cephalopholis urodeta) is a vivid Indo-Pacific species with distinctive white V-markings on the tail. Widely considered an aggressive ambush predator, it tends to consume any fish or crustacean small enough to fit in its mouth, making tankmate selection critical.
careNotes:
The Red Flag Grouper (Cephalopholis urodeta) is a medium-sized reef-associated grouper found across the tropical Indo-Pacific, from Christmas Island in the eastern Indian Ocean across the Pacific to French Polynesia and the Pitcairn Islands. It is commonly considered an intermediate-care species — undemanding in terms of water chemistry but demanding in tank volume, filtration capacity, and tankmate selection.

This species tends to behave as an ambush predator, holding station in caves and under overhangs and striking opportunistically at passing prey. It has been reported to consume any fish or crustacean small enough to fit in its mouth, including ornamental shrimp, small fish, and other invertebrates. Tankmates are generally best limited to robust, similarly-sized predators (large angelfish, tangs, triggerfish, moray eels). Aquarists frequently report the fish appears shy when first introduced and grows visibly bolder over weeks to months.

Given an adult size that typically reaches around 28 cm and a messy carnivorous diet, a minimum tank volume of around 700 L is broadly recommended, with ample live rock for hiding spots alongside open swimming space. Temperature recommendations vary across hobby sources and wild-occurrence data; a range of 23–27°C represents a practical middle ground that accommodates both captive and natural conditions.

The species is documented as a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite, consistent with the genus Cephalopholis and the broader grouper family Epinephelidae. Captive breeding has not been documented in the aquarium hobby. Wild-caught juveniles or subadults are typical in the trade.

Reef compatibility is generally rated as with caution — the species poses little direct threat to corals but will readily prey on ornamental shrimp, small fish, and other mobile invertebrates that share a reef display. It does not possess venomous spines, is not strongly nocturnal, and is not considered a significant escape risk for an enclosed marine system.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Cephalopholis urodeta has not been documented in hobby literature. As a protogynous hermaphrodite (documented at Iriomote Island, southern Japan), individuals begin life as female and the dominant fish in a group can transition to male with growth. Reproduction in the wild is reported as pelagic spawning over reef habitat.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary collection page; product page at https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/red-flag-grouper was used for detailed care values (180 gal min, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, aggressive, reef safe with caution).

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked species identity (Cephalopholis urodeta), adult size (11 in / ~28 cm), temperament (aggressive), reef compatibility (with caution), care level (intermediate), and minimum tank size (100 gal / ~379 L — notably smaller than LiveAquaria's 180 gal).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Epinephelidae / Perciformes; Forster, 1801), maximum size (28 cm TL), native range, depth (0–60 m, typical 3–15 m), and wild preferred temperature (25.2–29.3°C). Wild temperature runs higher than aquarium-hobby recommendations, likely reflecting tropical equatorial habitat vs. captive ranges.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Additional cross-check for adult size (10–11 in), minimum tank size (90 gal / ~340 L — the lowest of the surveyed sources), temperature (74–82°F / 23.3–27.8°C), pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.020–1.025, and decor preferences (live rock with large caves, ambush habitat). Tank size disagreement flagged in careNotes; the value used (700 L) was rounded upward toward the largest source recommendation per the cautious-larger rule for predators.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the hobby. In the wild, as a protogynous hermaphrodite, dominant females transition to male upon reaching sufficient size.
